Okay, after the pan- "To tell you the truth, to be honest, I He said nothing to her as he set her demic I went out to work and my brother- Sebastian Zapeta, the migrant accused don't remember," Zapeta replied. clothing ablaze witha lighter,thenin-acalld them and tol him that.

of fatally setting homeless woman Debrina Kawam on fire on a Coney Island subway train, pleaded not guilty Tuesday – even as ct papers evealed he said h, damn, that's me," when shown the horrific video of the attack that shocked the city.
When the detectives showed Zapeta video of himself as he lit Kawam ablaze, he responded with surprise, according to court documents.
Chilling video shows Zapeta sitting on a bench on the train platform as Kawam burned to death as she stood helplessly near the subway car's open door.
Up until his arrest, he was doing con- struction work in Queens, meeting his employer, who he refers to as "the Mex- ican," outside the boss' house in Jamaica before a job.
"Who is that person?" the Span- ish-speaking detective asks, and Zapeta responds, "Oh, damn, that's me."

Zapeta, a 33-year-old Guatemalan mi- grant living in a Brooklyn men's shelter, was arraigned before Brooklyn Supreme Court Justice Danny Chun on first-degree murder and arson charges. Chun ordered he remain held without bail until his next court appearance March 12.

Surveillance video later recovered by cops shows Zapeta inside the subway car and a police officer's body cam got a clear shot of his face, cops said. The NYPD quickly released images of the suspect to the media and a trio of high schoolers spotted him on another train later in the day and called 911, the com- missioner said.
at's you, rgh hecop contines and Zapeta responds, "Yes."
"He has a lot of work. He takes us to Brooklyn, or in Queens … or Wall Street," he told the detectives.
"Do you see what's happening now? Do you see what you're doing? You are setting that woman on fire. Do you re- member that or not?" the detective asks.

He finished work the day before the attack about 5:30 p.m., then after his em- ployer picked him up and drove him back to Queens, he went to a bar in Jamaica to buy beer and get drunk, he told detectives.

Until recently, 57-year-old Kawam was listed as a "Jane Doe" on the indictment against Zapeta. Authorities identified her last week and Assistant District Attorney Matthew Perry on Tuesday had the docu- ment amended to include her name.
"No, I really don't remember," Zapeta responds.
"But that's you, right?" the detective asks.

Zapeta was arrested by Customs and Border Patrol in Arizona on June 2, 2018, sources said, then removed by Immigra- tion and Customs Enforcement officials and returned to Guatemala. But he made his way back to the U.S. sometime after.
Zapeta had $178.80 on him when police arested him and told the cops processing him, "That's all I have."
"Yes," Zapeta replies, according to the court documents.

After Zapeta's arrest, he told a trio of NYPD detectives – one of whom translat- ed from Spanish– that he had no memory of what he'd done and that he often drank so much he would "erase the memory" of what happened and wake up at home.
When asked again why he did it, Za- peta responds, "I am very sorry. I didn't mean to. But I really don't know. I don't know what happened, but I'm very sorry for that woman."
He had to hand over his sweater and a bracelet and when the officers noted he was wearing two pairs of pants he ex- plained, "I have two because of the cold weather."

Zapeta told the detectives he came back to the U.S. illegally about five years ago, arriving in Queens with his wife's neph- ew. Relatives took him in but that living situation fell apart during the COVID-19 pandemic.

Zapeta approached Kawam as she slept in an F train stopped at the end of the line at Coney Island-Stillwell Ave. station at about 7:30 a.m. Dec. 22, fanned the fire with a shirt as the flames engulfed her, according to prosecutors.
His lawyer, Andrew Friedman, once again reserved the right to apply for bail at a later date.
The detectives confronted him with the video, with one telling him, "The best thing for us here, we want to know why
"I stayed with them, but in the brother-in-law was working with us, and he said that all the guys that worked there were infected with coronavirus."
With Emma Seiwell
